To solve the problem of finding how many ways the asterisks can be replaced, we must first understand the divisibility rules for the number twelve. A number is divisible by twelve if and only if it is divisible by both three and four. This means we need to satisfy two separate conditions simultaneously for the resulting eleven-digit number.

***

The condition for divisibility by four depends solely on the last two digits of the number. In our specific sequence, the last two digits are fixed as zero and two, forming the number zero two. Since two is divisible by four, any combination of the first nine digits will result in a number ending in zero two, which is always divisible by four. Therefore, the constraint regarding the final two digits is automatically satisfied regardless of the choices made for the asterisks.

***

The condition for divisibility by three requires that the sum of all digits in the number be a multiple of three. The known digits in the sequence are two, zero, one, six, zero, and two. Adding these together gives us a sum of eleven. We have five asterisks to fill, and each can be chosen from the set containing zero, two, four, seven, eight, or nine. The sum of the digits we choose must, when added to eleven, result in a total that is divisible by three.

***

We must analyze the remainders of the available digits when divided by three to simplify the calculation. The digit zero leaves a remainder of zero, two leaves a remainder of two, four leaves a remainder of one, seven leaves a remainder of one, eight leaves a remainder of two, and nine leaves a remainder of zero. Grouping these by their remainders, we see there are two digits with remainder zero, two digits with remainder one, and two digits with remainder two.

***

Since the sum of the known digits is eleven, which leaves a remainder of two when divided by three, the sum of the five chosen digits must leave a remainder of one to make the total sum divisible by three. We need to find the number of ways to select five digits from our available set such that their combined remainder is one. This involves calculating the number of combinations where the remainders sum to one, four, or seven, as these are the values congruent to one modulo three within the possible range.
